frontmatter.title: Classifying topoi in synthetic guarded domain theory: the universal property of multi-clock guarded recursion
frontmatter.meta: @inproceedings{palombi-sterling-2023, author = {Palombi, Daniele and Sterling, Jonathan}, booktitle = {Proceedings 38th Conference on Mathematical Foundations of Programming Semantics, {MFPS} 2022}, year = {2023}, month = feb, title = {Classifying topoi in synthetic guarded domain theory}, doi = {10.46298/entics.10323}, }
38th International Conference on Mathematical Foundations of Programming Semantics

mainmatter.p: Several different topoi have played an important role in the development and applications of synthetic guarded domain theory (SGDT), a new kind of synthetic domain theory that abstracts the concept of guarded recursion frequently employed in the semantics of programming languages. In order to unify the accounts of guarded recursion and coinduction, several authors have enriched SGDT with multiple “clocks” parameterizing different time-streams, leading to more complex and difficult to understand topos models. Until now these topoi have been understood very concretely qua categories of presheaves, and the logico-geometrical question of what theories these topoi classify has remained open. We show that several important topos models of SGDT classify very simple geometric theories, and that the passage to various forms of multi-clock guarded recursion can be rephrased more compositionally in terms of the lower bagtopos construction of Vickers and variations thereon due to Johnstone. We contribute to the consolidation of SGDT by isolating the universal property of multi-clock guarded recursion as a modular construction that applies to any topos model of single-clock guarded recursion.
I have served as an external advisor for the following bachelor students:
I have served as a committee-member for the following PhD students:
PhD student of Robert Harper.

Met with Fosco Loregian and Daniele Palombi on higher duploid theory. Fosco Loregian suggested that the immediate goal is to find a proper universal property for the Munch–Maccagnoni’s duploid construction as some kind of gluing or pushout, as it looks tantalizingly similar to a collage — it is almost the collage of the profunctor of a given adjunction, except that in the place where you usually have no morphisms, there are morphisms.

The obvious obstruction to a category-theoretic universal property is that it starts with categories and yields a non-category except in degenerate cases, but the idea of Fosco Loregian is that we might consider a duploid-theoretic universal property! As a category is a degenerate kind of duploid, we might consider the duploid construction as in fact happening in the category of duploids. This is not entirely nonsense, as duploids are models of an essentially algebraic theory and so can in principle be glued together.

Fosco Loregian has tried some naïve possibilities for such a gluing — but so far these have not worked. We concluded that we need to have a “shut up and calculate” meeting in order to actually come up with a conjecture as to what the gluing needs to be. We have set this meeting for 2023-10-05.

frontmatter.title: 2023-10-05 / higher duploid theory: “shut up and calculate”

mainmatter.p: Meeting with Fosco Loregian and Daniele Palombi.
Nathan Corbyn arrives in Cambridge. Meeting with Fosco Loregian and Daniele Palombi about higher duploid theory (“shut up and calculate” meeting).

frontmatter.title: 2023-10-05 / higher duploid theory: “shut up and calculate”

mainmatter.p: Meeting with Fosco Loregian and Daniele Palombi.
Received an email from Marco Paviotti suggesting a scientific meeting on guarded domain theory in 2024, to be held in Kent.
I had an idea for a potential student project (below); started a page to collect such project ideas.

One of the most common tasks when working in univalent foundations is to characterize the path space of a given type, i.e. establish a structure identity principle. Formalized SIP proofs often follow a baroque style involving identity systems and the even more complex displayed identity systems, etc. that sometimes obscure the very simple informal reasoning behind a given structure identity principle; I would like to develop a suite of lemmas and tactics that better reflect this informal reasoning.
To understand what I mean, let us consider how we might characterize the path spaces of morphisms in a co-slice A \downarrow \mathcal{U}.

Let A be a type; an A-algebra X is defined to be a type { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } equipped with a function { A } \xrightarrow{\alpha _A} { { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } }. A homomorphism { X } \xrightarrow{f} { Y } of A-algebras is defined to be a function { { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } } \xrightarrow{{ \left \lvert f \right \rvert }} { { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } } together with a homotopy \alpha _f \colon { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y. A homotopy H \colon f \sim g between homomorphisms { X } \xrightarrow{f,g} { Y } is given by a homotopy { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \colon { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } together with an assignment to each a:A of an identification \alpha _H a \colon { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \eta _g a = \eta _f a.

frontmatter.title: Structure identity principle for co-slice morphisms

mainmatter.p: Let A be a type and let { X } \xrightarrow{f} { Y } be a homomorphism of A-algebras. Assuming function extensionality, the type { \textstyle \sum _{ g \colon X \to Y }} g \sim f of homomorphisms homotopic to f is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We expand the dependent sum { \textstyle \sum _{ g \colon X \to Y }} g \sim f as a flat record telescope:
block.tex: \begin{bmatrix*} [l] { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } \to { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _H & \colon & { \textstyle \prod _{ a:A }} { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end{bmatrix*}

\begin{bmatrix*} [l] { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert X \right \rvert } \to { \left \lvert Y \right \rvert } \\ { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } & \colon & { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } \sim { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ \alpha _H & \colon & { \textstyle \prod _{ a:A }} { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end{bmatrix*}
block.p: By homotopy induction (function extensionality), the first two fields contract to a point with substitution { \left [ { \left \lvert g \right \rvert } : \equiv { \left \lvert f \right \rvert } ; { \left \lvert H \right \rvert } : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right ]}. What remains of our telescope is the following:
Zooming into the \alpha _H component, we simplify it and then zoom back out:

By dependent homotopy induction (also function extensionality), the remaining data collapse to a point with substitution { \left [ \alpha _g : \equiv \alpha _f; \alpha _H : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right ]}. The remaining telescope is empty, so we are done.

mainmatter.p: The steps performed in the proof of the SIP lemma for co-slice morphisms above are most naturally expressed at the level of a flat telescope (a context). The goal is always to contract a telescope to a point, and each step is either a rearrangement of the telescope, a “zooming in” step that adjusts a component of the telescope, or an application of some form of identity induction to kill off part of the telescope — path induction, homotopy induction, equivalence induction, etc. In general, we want to be able to easily point out the fragment of a telescope that contracts to a point, and then use the resulting substitution to modify the telescope and proceed further until the entire telescope is gone.

I believe that there is a reasonable and practical formal logic for doing such things, and that this logic can be implemented as a series of lemmas in homotopy type theory that can then be animated via tactics for interactive backward-inference proofs of contractibility.
I remembered to write down a sauce recipe I invented the other night.
This sauce can be served over rice, noodles, dumplings, or fish.

ol.li: Take some Szechuan peppercorns and dried chili flakes (or dried chilis that you have shattered) and crush them lightly in a mortar and pestle.
Toast the peppercorns and chili lightly in a hot pan.
Fry the peppercorns and chili in plenty of oil.
Fry some thinly chopped garlic without burning it.
Mix all this together (including the oil), and mix in some salt, mirin, rice vinegar, and (optionally) a very small amount of black vineger, to taste. Feel free to add more oil if needed.
Finely chop some coriander and mix it into the sauce.

mainmatter.p: I responded to Yue Niu’s email regarding his research statement.
